title: IRS Code 900 Meaning (Currently Not Collectible Status) – For Tax Professionals
description: IRS Code 900 means the account is Currently Not Collectible (CNC). Compare 530, 670, and 971, and summarize it in a client-ready report.
content:
IRS Transcript Codes • Built for 750,000+ U.S. Tax Professionals

IRS Code 900 Meaning

IRS Code 900 usually means the IRS marked the account Currently Not Collectible (CNC). This page explains what that changes (collection activity) and what it doesn’t (the assessed liability), plus how to summarize it cleanly for a client deliverable. Want to see what clients get? Open the sample report, then run your own transcript when you’re ready.

Try the parser (requires credits)

What is IRS Code 900?

Plain-English definition: IRS Code 900 generally indicates the IRS placed the account in Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status. In practical terms, it’s the IRS saying: “We are pausing active collection for now.”

Common transcript type
Account Transcript
Usually appears near
Codes 530, 670, 971
Typical context
Collection pause, hardship review, collectability review
Client-friendly summary
"The IRS paused active collection for now"

What CNC changes (and what it doesn’t)

What CNC usually changes

  • Collection activity: Active collection efforts are typically paused or reduced.
  • Immediate pressure: The IRS is often not pursuing new enforcement actions during CNC.
  • Review cycle: The IRS can re-check the taxpayer’s financial situation later.

What CNC usually does not change

  • Assessed liability: The tax assessed still exists.
  • Balance tracking: The module balance remains and should be monitored.
  • Interest/penalty exposure: Amounts may continue to accrue depending on the case.

If you need a quick refresher on how to read the surrounding codes and dates, use How to read IRS transcripts before you write the client summary.

When does Code 900 appear?

  • After a hardship/collectability review: CNC is commonly tied to the taxpayer’s current ability to pay.
  • After notices: You may see notice-related entries (like 971) before or around the status change.
  • After partial payments: A payment entry (like 670) can appear in the timeline, but CNC is about collectability, not “paid in full.”

What should a tax professional do next?

  1. Balance: Confirm the current module balance and whether it’s increasing.
  2. Dates: Capture the posting date for Code 900 and any nearby notices or payments.
  3. Documentation: Write a short summary that CNC pauses collection but doesn’t erase the debt.
  4. Next step: Set a review cadence to re-check transcripts for status changes or new notices.

Example: how this looks in a parsed report

Below is a simplified view of how Code 900 can appear in a client-ready transcript report with context:

Collection Status Timeline (Simplified)
Code 971 – Notice issued
Date: 02/12/2024
Code 670 – Payment received
Amount: $250 • Date: 03/01/2024
Code 900 – Account currently not collectible (CNC)
Date: 03/18/2024

FAQ

What does IRS Code 900 mean on a tax transcript?

IRS Code 900 generally means the IRS placed the account in Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status, pausing active collection due to collectability factors.

Does IRS Code 900 mean the debt is forgiven?

No. CNC is not forgiveness. The liability remains, and the IRS can re-evaluate collectability later.

How is Code 900 related to Code 530?

Both can appear in CNC scenarios. Use dates and surrounding entries to confirm what changed and when.
